abstract: In 1972, Erich Clar introduced the aromatic $\pi$-sextet rule, providing a qualitative framework for understanding the aromatic character and stability of benzenoid hydrocarbons. Forty years since its inception, this simple yet powerful model remains a cornerstone of organic chemistry, widely validated by both experimental observations and theoretical calculations. This review explores the enduring relevance of Clar’s rule, highlighting its ability to predict a range of physical and chemical properties, including bond lengths, resonance energies, and chemical reactivity in polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). While originally developed for small molecular species, the rule has more recently shown success in describing complex carbon nanostructures such as graphene nanoribbons and nanotubes. Here, we discuss the fundamental principles of the $\pi$-sextet rule, its performance against modern aromaticity indices, and its recent application in explaining the strength of resonance-assisted hydrogen bonds and the emergence of biradical character in large acenes. Despite some qualitative limitations, Clar’s rule remains an essential tool for the intuitive analysis of $\pi$-conjugated systems.
